For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to Investment Management & Financial Services | BlackRock for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.75%; for Investor A shares: 1.00%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.16% (Institutional shares) and 2.09% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The health care, apartment, and single-family housing sectors drove the fund’s underperformance relative to its benchmark. The office, warehouse, and lodging sectors aided relative to returns.
  • During the quarter, the fund had overweight positions in the data center, tower, and cold storage sectors, and underweight holdings in the health care, self-storage, and manufactured home sectors.

Contributors

Detractors
At a security level, underweight exposures to Digital Realty (DLR) (+41 basis points) and Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) (+19bps), and an overweight allocation to EPR Properties (EPR) (+19bps) contributed to relative performance. At a sector level, an underweight holding in offices (+19bps), and overweight positions in warehouses (+14bps) and lodging (+12bps) were beneficial.

At a security level, overweight holdings in Federal Realty (FRT) (-29bps) and Iron Mountain (IRM) (-26bps), and an underweight allocation to Crown Castle (CCI)(-27bps) were detrimental. At a sector level, underweight allocations to health care (-31bps) and single-family housing (-11bps), and an overweight exposure to apartments (-12bps) hampered relative returns.

Further insight

Despite trade policy uncertainty, long-term real estate sector fundamentals remain positive. Supply constraints are driving pricing power, with higher interest rates impacting supply deliveries since 2022. This is expected to benefit real estate investment trust pricing power through 2025. Listed real estate valuations are attractive and cheap relative to broader equities, in our view. Stimulus measures across Europe and Asia could act as key catalysts and bolster regional economic growth. Secular trends are accelerating, with increased protectionism likely to speed up development in industries such as logistics and digital real estate. Real estate securities compare favorably with the broad equity market, which has declined since the U.S. administration’s tariff announcements.

★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

Institutional shares rated against 213 Us Fund Real Estate Funds, as of 3/31/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics.††

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% in equity investments engaged primarily in real estate industries inside the United States and up to 20% outside the United States (deriving at least 50% of revenues or profits from commercial, industrial or residential real estate industries or with 50% of its assets in real estate interests).

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Investing in small- and mid-cap companies may entail greater risk than large-cap companies, due to shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes. Non-diversification of investments means that more assets are potentially invested in fewer securities than if investments were diversified, so risk is increased because each investment has a greater effect on performance. Concentrating investments in single sector means that performance will be more susceptible to factors affecting that sector and more volatile than funds that invest in many different sectors. If interest rates rise, the value of convertible securities usually falls. Convertible securities are subject to the risk that the issuer will not pay interest or dividends and their market value may change based on their credit rating or the perception of their creditworthiness. A convertible security also is subject to the same risks as common stock. The main risk of real estate securities is that their value may go down depending on economies, vacancy rates, interest rates, tenant bankruptcies, amount of new construction in an area, laws and regulations and real estate maintenance and improvement. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Many real estate-related securities issuers are highly leveraged, increasing the risk of such securities.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Global Real Estate Index series is designed to represent general trends in eligible real estate equities worldwide. 2 The information shows you how the Fund’s performance has varied for the periods since inception and provides some indication of the risks of investing in the Fund. The table compares the Fund’s performance to that of the FTSE EPRA Nareit United States Index. Effective June 1, 2020, the Fund changed its benchmark to the MSCI US REIT Index, as BlackRock considers this benchmark to be more representative of the sectors in which the Fund invests.†† The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product s monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled Us Fund Real Estate funds over the following time periods:213 in the last 3 years, 196 in the last 5 years and 148 in the last 10 years. With respect to these Us Fund Real Estate funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 3 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. data.title: Retail Sales Inch Up 0.1% In April, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Census Bureau's Advance Retail Sales Report for April showed consumer spending inched up last month, with head sales rising 0.1%. This comes on the heels of March's 1.7% surge in spending and was higher than the 0.0% forecast.

For an inflation-adjusted perspective on retail sales, take a look at our Real Retail Sales commentary.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for April 2025, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $724.1 billion, up 0.1 percent (±0.5 percent)* from the previous month, and up 5.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from April 2024. Total sales for the February 2025 through April 2025 period were up 4.8 percent (±0.5 percent) from the same period a year ago. The February 2025 to March 2025 percent change was revised from up 1.5 percent (±0.5 percent) to up 1.7 percent (±0.2 percent).Retail trade sales were down 0.1 percent (±0.5 percent)* from March 2025, and up 4.7 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Motor vehicle and parts dealers were up 9.4 percent (±1.8 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 7.8 percent (±1.8 percent) from April 2024.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent-up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 5.2% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) rose 0.1% in April. This is down from March's 0.8% reading and lower than the expected 0.3% growth in core sales.

Core retail sales are up 4.2% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials as well as food services & drinking places.

The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases unexpectedly fell 0.2% in April. This is down from March's 0.4% reading and lower than the expected 0.3% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 5.0% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Refer to (800)-441-7762 for most recent month-end performance.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted a return of -10.69% (Class I shares) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were investment decisions in the communication services, consumer discretionary, and real estate sectors. The largest detractors were investment decisions in the information technology (IT), consumer staples, and industrials sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the communication services and health care sectors, and reduced its exposures to the IT and industrials sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

Stock selection in the communication services sector, namely in the interactive media & services industry, was the largest contributor to relative performance. In the consumer discretionary sector, stock selection in the automobiles industry proved beneficial. Lastly, security selection in the real estate management & development industry within the real estate sector boosted relative results.

The largest detractor was security selection in IT sector. Notably, stock selection in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry dampened relative results. In the consumer staples sector, an avoidance of the beverages industry hindered performance. Another meaningful detractor included security selection in the industrials sector, namely an overweight allocation to the electrical equipment industry.

Further insight

During the quarter, U.S. equities faced elevated uncertainty, with sharp daily trading moves and heavy macroeconomic influence, akin to emerging markets. Investors had to navigate a rapidly shifting landscape due to geopolitical developments and changing growth and interest rate expectations. Fluid tariff implications add headline risk and supply-chain pressures, which are impacting corporate margins and global trade. Despite this, the U.S. economy starts from a solid footing, with strong corporate and household balance sheets, high profit margins, and a healthy labor market. Potential policy tailwinds, such as deregulation and tax cuts, could boost business confidence and investment activity.

Investment approach

data.title: Palantir Will Likely Win The AI Race From America
data.description:
I was wrong and the Markets were Right

If you've read Intelligent Investor by Benjamin Graham, then you might remember learning about Mr. Market in Chapter 8. The chapter was rightfully titled, "Chapter 8: The Investor and Market Fluctuations." When combined with Chapter 20 about the Margin of Safety, investors quickly learn the difference between price and valuation.

In March, I wrote about the high valuation and risks with Palantir's stock (NASDAQ:PLTR). The stock was coming off a recent high of $124 from their February earnings. The Trump Bump pushed every stock to new highs and the market peaked near the end of February. I published an earnings update shortly after because I thought $80 was a good price to hold the stock. I didn't have a strong conviction to buy because PLTR trades at a very high price-to-earnings multiple of 200x.

But I did recommend to hold at $80 because the stock achieved a new price floor. This recommendation worked out until the end of April, after Palantir delivered even more impressive results. Today, I'll provide an update on the stock to reiterate my Hold recommendation. If you already own PLTR, keep the position and maybe trim off some gains as the price rises. At the moment, it looks like Palantir's stock will remain above $100, so let's dive into the updates.

National Security is America's #1 priority

Before we begin, I recommend reading Alex Karp's Q1 shareholder letter. Alex provides the typical executive summaries, but it also provides insights to Palantir's future: a clear commitment to the U.S. Defense and intelligence agencies.

Now, in recent history, this level of commitment to the America-First agenda has been frowned upon by shareholders and customers. Typically, customers could be persuaded too, but in Palantir's case a majority of their revenue comes from government agencies. You might remember that in 2018, Google cancelled Project Maven with the Pentagon. Google's employees were against the company using A.I. to enhance defense technologies for the U.S. government.

This was a controversial moment in U.S. history because it limited the U.S. government's ability to access the best technologies. But it also created an opportunity for companies like Palantir and Anduril to capture market share from the prime defense contractors.

Which is why I tuned into the Hill & Valley Forum before Palantir's earnings call. The forum is designed to enhance the public-private partnerships between American companies and the government. Numerous defense-related topics are discussed, one of the main ones being the A.I. race. I recommend watching Alex Karp's forum interview to learn more.

The takeaway from Karp's interview was Palantir's role in A.I. for U.S. Defense. Besides Anduril, which is a fast-growing startup, Palantir is the only public defense tech stock working on cutting-edge A.I. The most important element to be aware of is that Palantir is an American-first company and aligned with our core values. This alignment is non-traditional when you realize most tech companies are steadfast on globalization. Palantir remains the best bet that America will dominate and lead the A.I. race in the future.

Palantir earnings review

Now if you own Palantir stock, then you would have seen their strong first-quarter results for 2025, which were exceptional. Revenue increased by 39% year-over-year to $884 million, a majority of which came from U.S. commercial and government revenue growth. Palantir's customer count also grew by 39% year-over-year, along with net income, cash flow and margins all growing.

Full-year and second quarter guidance has been raised across the board. For me, the most impressive factor is the rate at which Palantir is growing as a multibillion dollar company. They closed 139 deals worth at least $1 million, including 51 deals of at least $5 million and 31 deals of over $10 million. Closing eight-figure contracts at scale suggests the company is firing on all cylinders.

Karp highlights Palantir's operational efficiency and its ability to attract and retain top talent, which is why growth continues to compound. The company's deep commitment to developing cutting-edge solutions, combined with motivated employees, is a serious competitive advantage.

The xAI Partnership is overlooked by Investors

There are only two A.I. models I use on a regular basis: xAI's Grok and Google's Gemini. Gemini has the largest token context window, so it consumes the most amount of data and has the multi-modal offering. But for daily context, I use xAI's Grok every single day for real-time news. Grok is also iterating its core product at great speed, so I'm using some version of Grok 3.5 and SuperGrok online.

Even though OpenAI's ChatGPT has a first-mover advantage, I don't think enough people understand the full A.I. landscape. xAI was founded by Elon Musk, who raised $12 billion in funding and built an A.I. data center with 300k Nvidia GPUs, which is the biggest of its kind. This has made xAI a serious contender in the A.I. race. And now xAI is partnering with Palantir.

I noticed this news the day after Palantir's earnings call, when Alex Karp was being interviewed alongside Thomas Tull, cofounder of TWG Global. They mentioned that the combined partnership of xAI, Palantir and TWG, would accelerate A.I. adoption in the financial services industry. Given the rate at which xAI is growing its Nvidia GPU clusters, I think this partnership will help Palantir meet its Q2 2025 expectations of between $934 million and $938 million. This will also begin to diversify more of Palantir's revenue away from the government, and towards the commercial sector. I don't think this deal is well understood by the market.

How to Value Palantir

Again, this is a growth stock in a league of its own. The probability of Palantir becoming the next trillion dollar public company is more likely every day. But there will need to be several key catalysts before the trillion dollar milestone is achieved.

Right now, I think Palantir will be able to deliver double-digit growth for 2025 and 2026. These long-term contracts will continue to compound, particularly within the commercial sector. However, this is not an indication to buy more Palantir stock and increase your exposure. Instead, investors will be better off holding their current positions steady.

I don't have a solid valuation method for this stock because it continues to trade on momentum. In March, I thought $80 was the new floor, but it looks like the price has risen to +$100. As an investor, the major risk here is the permanent loss of capital if you buy the stock at a very high price. Palantir continues to trade at a 200x P/E multiple, which means any breaking news will introduce price volatility. In your portfolio, you probably don't want to have more 3-5% exposed to PLTR unless you are willing to stomach more market fluctuations.

data.title: AT&T: Wait Before Buying More (Technical Analysis, Downgrade)
data.description:
Intro

At the end of January, I reiterated my buy rating for AT&T Inc. (NYSE:T) and the stock has gained around 10% since then. However, I would encourage investors to refrain from buying more for now. In the below analysis, I determine that most technical indications show the stock may experience a period of weakness. For the fundamentals, Q1 results were resilient, with operating cash flows being a highlight. While the P/CF ratio has expanded to reflect the solid financials, it remains far below the sector median, indicating undervaluation. The fundamental setup remains attractive in my view, but the deterioration of the technical outlook leads me to downgrade the stock to a hold for now.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

AT&T stock was in a longer-term uptrend starting in mid-January, but in early March, that uptrend line was unable to hold. Resistance was experienced at that former uptrend line in late March and early April. A nearer term uptrend line then formed from the early April low, but the recent pullback has also broken this trend line. Therefore, the stock is not in much of a trend at this moment.

Support

There are three significant support levels that are relatively nearby. The first is at 26 as that level was important support back in early March as well as in late April. Moving down, the 25 area was resistance back in late January, but was support in March and April. Lastly, the 24 level could be support, as it was resistance twice in early December.

Resistance

In addition to the former near term uptrend line, there are three very relevant resistance levels. The nearest one is at 27.54 as a downside gap occurred there just a few days ago on quite heavy volume. The next source of resistance would be at about 27.9. That level was key resistance in early March as well as for a large part of April. Finally, 28.52 could be an obstacle as it was a small downside gap that occurred on the highest bearish volume of April.

Takeaway

I believe the chart is a slightly negative one for AT&T. While the stock is not in much of a trend, the fact that it has broken both a longer-term and near term downtrend may be a sign of weakness. Furthermore, the resistance levels are more condensed and are closer to the stock, and so the high 20s area may be a tough barrier for AT&T to break above.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average [SMA]

The 50-day SMA has been above the 200-day SMA for the past six months to show that there has been general long-term bullishness in the stock. However, there are still reasons for caution. The 50-day SMA's trajectory has flattened and its deviation with the 200-day SMA has decreased to show that upward momentum is on the decline. Furthermore, the stock has broken beneath the 50-day SMA as a sign of weakness.

Exponential Moving Average [EMA]

The 9 day EMA just broke below the 21 day EMA for a bearish crossover. This shows that the near term has become negative for AT&T. The 9 day EMA's trajectory is severely downwards, reflecting strong bearish momentum currently. After contributing as support in late April and early May, both EMA lines may now be a source of resistance for the stock.

Bollinger Bands

AT&T was able to truly break above the Bollinger Bands midline at the end of April after a few failed attempts. This confirmed the near term uptrend but when the stock gapped below that trend line, it also fell below the midline. Therefore, this was a confirmation signal that the uptrend is no longer in effect. The stock did reach the lower band on the previous trading day, and so an oversold bounce may occur.

Takeaway

I would say that the outlook conveyed by these moving averages is bearish. The SMAs show that long term upward momentum is receding, the EMAs indicate that the near term has become bearish, and the Bollinger Bands have confirmed the end of the uptrend. Therefore, further losses are more likely from here.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD showed negative divergence at the stock's early April peak, as the indicator made a much lower high than back in early March. This shows that the top may be in for AT&T. In recent days, the MACD had a bearish crossover with the signal line, showing the bears are back. As shown by the weak histogram prior to this crossover, the bulls were never really able to pull away.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

Like the MACD, the RSI also displays bearish divergence at the early April stock peak, as the indicator got nowhere near its early March peak. The RSI has, in fact, been in a downtrend since that peak, and so further weakness may occur. Currently, the indicator has a reading of 42.21, showing that the bears have fair control over the stock.

Stochastics

The stochastics had a bearish crossover earlier this month, with the %K breaking below the %D. Both lines have since plunged, with the %K now below 20 while the %D has broken beneath 50. This shows that the bears are rapidly tightening their grasp on the stock. As shown above, this is one of the steepest declines for the indicator in the past six months, and so sentiment has shifted rather quickly.

Takeaway

The overall negativity continues into the indicators in my view. The MACD had a bearish crossover, the RSI continues its downtrend, and both stochastics lines have plunged as signs of weakness. Furthermore, the MACD and RSI both showed negative divergence, and so the longer-term peak for AT&T is likely to be in.

Fibonacci Retracement

Using the January low and the early April high, Fibonacci Retracement levels are shown above. During the April plunge, AT&T found support at the 50% retracement. It also found support at the 38.2% retracement later that month. The stock had rallied back to above the 23.6% retracement earlier this month, but it recently gapped back below that level. If the pullback continues, the 38.2% retracement in the low 26s price area will likely be support again. While the majority of other technical indications have been bearish, the fact that the stock has thus far held above the 50% retracement shows that there is potential AT&T could avoid a full retracement of its gains.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

AT&T reported their 2025 Q1 earnings on April 23rd and showed resilient results as a whole. They generated total revenues of $30.6 billion, up 1.99% over the year ago quarter. This figure was able to beat expectations by $264.01 million. As you can see above, revenue growth has been quite stable for the past few years. In their Mobility division, they saw operating revenues grow 4.7% to $21.57 billion as there was strength in service revenue and also equipment revenue. Their Business Wireline division, on the other hand, saw weaker results with operating revenues declining 9.1% to $4.468 billion as there was weakness in legacy and other transitional services. Finally, for their Consumer Wireline division, they reported operating revenue growth of 5.1% to $3.522 billion as there was strong broadband revenue growth. As for company-wide EPS, AT&T posted an adjusted figure of $0.51, up 6.25% from the year ago quarter's $0.48. This came in-line with analyst estimates. Lastly, for operating cash flows, they saw growth of 19.90% YoY to $9.0 billion. As shown above, this growth rate is above the multiyear average and was a nice QoQ acceleration.

Valuation

Price To Operating Cash Flows Ratio [P/CF]

The P/CF ratio has really been on a tear since late 2023 as it has climbed from around 2.8 all the way to a current reading of 4.721. I believe this multiple potentially shows around fair valuation of AT&T relative to past growth data. As discussed above, operating cash flows growth saw a nice QoQ acceleration and is at above average levels. Furthermore, the 19.9% growth rate is one of the highest seen in the past three years. Therefore, the P/CF ratio being far above average and near the multiyear peak is not surprising given the fundamental strength of the company. This multiple may, in fact, show undervaluation when compared to peers. The Communication Services sector has a median P/CF ratio of 7.41 as of this writing, and so the stock is at a 36% discount according to this measure. With AT&T being one of the biggest names in the sector and growth in their cash flows being at healthy levels, I believe this shows undervaluation in the stock.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was determined that most technicals show a weak outlook. Despite the Fibonacci Retracement showing that a full retracement is unlikely at this point, near term losses may continue for AT&T. As for the fundamentals, Q1 earnings were overall strong in my view. Impressive operating cash flow growth shows that the company is still performing well. While the P/CF ratio has risen to reflect this financial strength, it remains far below the sector median, suggesting undervaluation versus peers. However, while I believe the fundamentals remain desirable for AT&T, I also believe that the weak technicals justify a hold rating for now.

For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.80%; for Investor A shares: 1.05%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -13.35% (Institutional shares) and -13.43% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were security selection in the financials sector, positioning in the industrials sector, and investment decisions in the consumer discretionary sector. The largest detractor from relative returns was stock selection in the information technology (IT) and communication services sectors, as well as positioning in the utilities sector.
  • The largest exposures were in the IT, industrials, and health care sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the financials and IT sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and materials sectors.

Contributors

Detractors
Stock selection in the real estate sector was the largest contributor to relative performance. Notably, security selection in the real estate management & development industry proved beneficial. Investment decisions in the consumer staples sector, namely in the textiles, apparel & luxury goods industry, boosted relative results. Lastly, in the consumer staples sector, an avoidance of the personal care products industry added value.

The largest detractor was stock selection in the IT sector. Notably, stock selection in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry dampened relative results. In the health care sector, underweight positioning in the health care providers & services industry hindered performance. Another meaningful detractor included security selection in the industrials sector, particularly an overweight allocation to the electrical equipment industry.

Further insight

During the quarter, U.S. equities faced elevated uncertainty, with sharp daily trading moves and heavy macroeconomic influence, akin to emerging markets. Investors had to navigate a rapidly shifting landscape due to geopolitical developments and changing growth and interest rate expectations. Fluid tariff implications add headline risk and supply-chain pressures, which are impacting corporate margins and global trade. Despite this, the U.S. economy starts from a solid footing, with strong corporate and household balance sheets, high profit margins, and a healthy labor market. Potential policy tailwinds, such as deregulation and tax cuts, could boost business confidence and investment activity.

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its net assets in equity securities issued by U.S. mid- capitalization growth companies that fund management team believes have above-average earnings growth potential. Although a universal definition of mid-capitalization companies does not exist, the fund generally defines these companies as those with market capitalizations comparable in size to companies in the Russell Midcap Growth Index.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market condi- tions. Investing in mid-cap companies may entail greater risk than large-cap companies, due to shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Performance may be attributable to unusually high IPO profits. There is no guarantee this level of performance will be repeated. IPO securities have no trading history and the price may be volatile.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

data.title: Twin Hospitality's FAT Brands Liquidity Falls As Preferred Dividends Suspended
data.description:
FAT Brands (NASDAQ:FAT) (NASDAQ:FATBB) suspended its quarterly dividend to common shareholders at the start of 2025, following up with a suspension of its monthly paid distribution to its preferred shareholders (NASDAQ:FATBP) a few months later. I warned about the looming cuts in my prior coverage of the ticker. Fundamentally, the distributions were unsustainable for a company that ended its fiscal 2025 first quarter with long-term debt of $1.2 billion, a roughly 23x multiple of its current market and far in excess of cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $41.5 million as of the end of the quarter. FAT ended the quarter with a total stockholders' deficit of $500 million, up $45 million sequentially, as its total liabilities reached $1.77 billion against total assets of $1.27 billion. With total assets 68.8% constituted by goodwill and intangible assets, the liquidation value of FAT's balance sheet, as per a crude total assets minus goodwill, could be much lower. FAT completed its spinoff of its Twin Peaks and Smokey Bones restaurant chain (NASDAQ:TWNP) back in January but retains 95% ownership in the publicly traded entity. The performance of FAT is invariably interlocked with the performance of TWNP.

FAT is unprofitable, burning through its dwindling cash reserve, and faces a mountain of debt that threatens to force the company into tough questions around how to possibly restructure its debt. FAT generated first quarter revenue of $142.01 million, down 6.6% over its year-ago comp and a miss by $6.54 million on consensus estimates. Net income was negative at $46 million, up from $38.3 million a year ago. FAT trades on a 0.085x multiple to its annualized first quarter revenue at its current market cap.

Twin Peaks Performance

TWNP generated first-quarter revenue of $87.1 million, down 5.4% over its year-ago comp, and with a net loss of $12.1 million growing from $9.2 million a year ago. TWNP currently trades on a $302 million market cap, around a 0.87x price-to-sales multiple to annualized first quarter revenue. The market seems to have extended an olive branch to TWNP in terms of its valuation despite the company suffering from the same heavy debt burden, falling liquidity position, and negative free cash flow that FAT has seen its commons get weighed down by. The company's interest expenses at $10.8 million represent a core headwind, as long-term debt stood at $413 million as of the end of the first quarter. Cash and cash equivalents were $7.15 million, with free cash burn during the quarter at $9.7 million. TWNP will likely have to lean on equity sales to make up its liquidity gap.

FAT's Twin Peaks Securitization Notes, Liquidity, And Free Cash Burn

FAT held $402.3 million of Twin Peaks securitization notes at book value as of the end of the first quarter. These are secured by a security interest in substantially all the assets of FAT Brands Twin Peaks I, a subsidiary created in connection with the October 2021 acquisition of Twin Peaks. FAT currently pays a coupon of 7% on 69.05% of the aggregate TWNP securitization notes. Critically, while the legal final maturity date of the notes is October 26, 2054, the anticipated repayment date is much sooner on October 25, 2027. Why? If the notes are not repaid or refinanced by the anticipated repayment date, then FAT will face additional interest on the outstanding balance of each tranche of the notes at a rate of 5% per year. Further, TWNP is required to deposit 75% of the net proceeds of each qualified equity offering of its common shares into a segregated account to be used for repayment of the notes until an aggregate of $75 million has been repaid. TWNP currently has a market cap of $302 million, less than the aggregate number of notes owed by FAT.

FAT is now in a position where is operations are fundamentally unprofitable, with the unrestricted portion of its cash position in free fall. This cash position was $12.15 million as of the end of the first quarter, amongst its lowest level in over 3 years, as quarterly free cash flow notched another negative quarter at $17.76 million. FAT has made up this liquidity gap in the past by adding more debt, with its total long-term debt up 40% over the last 3 years. This becomes increasingly unsustainable the further the equity component of the company declines.

Negative shareholders' equity has deepened further as near-term liquidity transactions threaten to ramp up this trend. FAT has access to $73.6 million from issued but not sold fixed-rate secured notes and $75.6 million from repurchased but not resold fixed-rate secured notes. The company considers the proceeds from these to be sufficient for operations through the next 12 months. There will still have to be underlying investor demand for these.

What is the upside and risk to the bearish thesis here? TWNP is able to refinance its debt at favourable rates and is also able to reduce cash burn from current levels. While these risks, will not reinstate the common share or preferred dividends in the near to medium term. They could improve sentiment on both. The preferreds have collapsed to $3.60 per share following the dividend suspension, around 14 cents on the dollar. The company is effectively burning through the last of its liquidity sources as it records consecutive quarters of negative free cash flow. I don't see these as having any latent recovery value. FAT and TWNP both remain a sell.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
